2:0.rendering: Then Naomi her mother-in-law said to her, "My daughter, should I not seek a resting place for you that will be good for you?"
2:0.note: Naomi uses the same Hebrew word for "resting place" that Boaz later uses for Ruth finding shelter under Yahweh's wings in 2:12, hinting at divine provision through human kindness.

2:1.rendering: Now, is not Boaz our relative, whose young women you were with? Look, he is winnowing barley on the threshing floor tonight.
2:1.note: The Hebrew word for "relative" here is the same "go'el" redeemer used later in the story, quietly signaling Boaz's role in the larger family-rescue drama.

2:2.rendering: So wash yourself, put on perfume, and put on your best cloak. Then go down to the threshing floor. Do not let the man know you are there until he has finished eating and drinking.
2:2.note: The Hebrew verb for "put on" (literally "place upon yourself") echoes how God "placed" garments on Adam and Eve in Eden, a quiet hint of redemption through human initiative under covenant kindness.

2:3.rendering: When he lies down, notice the place where he is lying. Then go in, uncover his feet, and lie down there. He will tell you what you should do.
2:3.note: The Hebrew for "uncover his feet" carries a playful double meaning that echoes the same phrase used when Noah's nakedness was exposed in Genesis 9.

2:4.rendering: She said, "Everything you have said to me, I will do."
2:4.note: Ruth's brief reply echoes her earlier pledge to Naomi in 1:16, showing her decisive commitment to the risky plan.

2:5.rendering: She went down to the threshing floor and did everything her mother-in-law had commanded her.
2:5.note: The Hebrew verb for "did" echoes the same word used when Boaz earlier "did" all that Ruth had asked, quietly mirroring their growing covenant faithfulness.

2:6.rendering: So Boaz ate and drank, and his heart was merry. Then he went to lie down at the end of the grain heap. Ruth came quietly, uncovered his feet, and lay down.
2:6.note: In the ancient world, "uncovering feet" was a bold, culturally loaded gesture that echoes the protective language of spreading a garment over someone in verse 9.

2:7.rendering: In the middle of the night the man was startled, turned over, and there was a woman lying at his feet.
2:7.note: The Hebrew word for "startled" carries the same shock as when Boaz earlier "noticed" Ruth in the field, quietly echoing how God is stirring this story forward.

1:0.rendering: Now Naomi had a relative on her husband's side, a man of standing and substance from the clan of Elimelek, whose name was Boaz.
1:0.note: The Hebrew word for "relative" here (mod'a) carries the nuance of a potential redeemer, quietly setting the stage for the kinsman-redeemer theme that will unfold.

1:1.rendering: And Ruth the Moabite said to Naomi, "Let me go to the field and glean among the ears of grain after him in whose sight I shall find favor." And she said to her, "Go, my daughter."
1:1.note: The Hebrew word for "favor" (chen) echoes the "kindness" (chesed) that will drive this whole story, hinting that Ruth is stepping into covenant loyalty in a foreign field.

1:2.rendering: So she went out, entered the field, and gleaned behind the harvesters. And she happened to come to the portion of the field belonging to Boaz, who was from the clan of Elimelech.
1:2.note: The Hebrew word for "happened" carries a wink of divine providence, as this "chance" arrival sets the stage for the clan's redemption.

1:3.rendering: Just then Boaz arrived from Bethlehem. He said to the harvesters, "The LORD be with you." They answered him, "The LORD bless you."
1:3.note: This greeting exchange echoes the ancient blessing formula that will later frame the entire book's resolution, linking Boaz's field to Bethlehem's coming king.

1:4.rendering: Then Boaz said to his young man who was set over the reapers, "Whose young woman is this?"
1:4.note: The Hebrew word for "young man" here echoes the same term used for Ruth as a "young woman," creating a subtle literary pairing that hints at their future connection.

1:5.rendering: The servant who was set over the reapers answered, "She is the Moabite young woman who came back with Naomi from the country of Moab."
1:5.note: The repeated phrase "Moabite young woman" echoes the opening of the book, quietly underscoring Ruth's outsider status even as she steps onto Boaz's field.

1:6.rendering: She said, 'Please let me glean and gather among the sheaves after the reapers.' So she came and has continued from early morning until now, except that she rested a little in the house."
1:6.note: The young man's report uses precise harvest vocabulary, echoing the laws of Leviticus 19 and 23 that commanded Israelites to leave gleanings for the poor and the foreigner.

1:7.rendering: Then Boaz said to Ruth, "Listen, my daughter. Do not go to glean in another field. Do not even leave this one. Instead, stay close to my young women here."
1:7.note: Boaz's command to "stay close" uses the Hebrew verb that echoes the same word for Ruth's earlier pledge to "cling" to Naomi in 1:14, quietly hinting at covenant loyalty coming full circle.

3:0.rendering: Now Boaz had gone up to the gate and sat down there. And look, the kinsman-redeemer whom Boaz had spoken of came passing by. Boaz said, "Turn aside, friend, sit down here." So he turned aside and sat down.
3:0.note: The Hebrew word for the unnamed kinsman is literally "such-and-such," a playful placeholder that keeps him anonymous while the true redeemer steps forward.

3:1.rendering: Boaz took ten men from the elders of the town and said, "Sit down here." And they sat down.
3:1.note: In ancient Israelite custom, ten elders formed a legal assembly to witness and validate a transaction at the town gate.

3:2.rendering: Then Boaz said to the closer relative, "Naomi, who has come back from the fields of Moab, is selling the portion of the field that belonged to our brother Elimelech."
3:2.note: The Hebrew word for "selling" here carries the double sense of relinquishing a field that could be redeemed, echoing the family land laws of Leviticus 25.

3:3.rendering: I thought I should uncover your ear and say, 'Buy it in the presence of those seated here and in the presence of the elders of my people.' If you will redeem it, redeem it. But if you will not redeem it, tell me so that I may know. For there is no one besides you to redeem it, and I am next after you.
3:3.note: The Hebrew phrase 'uncover your ear' is an idiom for privately informing someone, echoing how God reveals plans to his servants.

3:4.rendering: Then Boaz said, "On the day you acquire the field from Naomi's hand, you also acquire Ruth the Moabite, the widow of the dead man, in order to raise up the name of the dead upon his inheritance."
3:4.note: The Hebrew word for "acquire" here echoes the language of redemption, linking this moment back to the kinsman-redeemer theme that runs through the book.

3:5.rendering: The kinsman-redeemer said, "I cannot redeem it for myself, or I would ruin my own inheritance. You redeem my right of redemption for yourself, for I cannot redeem it."
3:5.note: The nearer kinsman bows out because the cost of redeeming Naomi's land would also mean supporting Ruth and her future sons, diluting his own family line.

3:6.rendering: Now this was the custom in former times in Israel concerning redemption and exchange: to confirm any transaction, a man would take off his sandal and give it to the other party. This was the way of attesting in Israel.
3:6.note: The sandal-offering ceremony echoes the land-redemption laws of Deuteronomy 25, turning a simple property deal into a public, memorable act of integrity.

3:7.rendering: So the redeemer said to Boaz, "Buy it for yourself," and he took off his sandal and gave it to him.
3:7.note: The sandal transfer echoes an ancient custom for confirming land redemption, turning a legal formality into a vivid picture of one man stepping aside for another.

0:0.rendering: In the days when the judges ruled, there was a famine in the land, and a man from Bethlehem in Judah went to live as a foreigner in the country of Moab, he, his wife, and his two sons.
0:0.note: The Hebrew word for "live as a foreigner" (gur) echoes Abraham's own sojourning, signaling that this family's story is part of the larger covenant drama.

0:1.rendering: The man's name was Elimelek, and his wife's name was Naomi. They had two sons, Mahlon and Kilion. They were Ephrathites from Bethlehem in Judah, and they came to the fields of Moab and settled there.
0:1.note: These Ephrathite names carry a bitter echo: Elimelek means 'my God is king,' yet famine drives them from the house of bread (Bethlehem) into enemy territory.

0:2.rendering: Elimelek died, and Naomi was left with her two sons.
0:2.note: Ancient Israelite culture viewed a widow without a husband or grown sons as utterly vulnerable, setting up the desperate stakes of the whole story.

0:3.rendering: They lifted up for themselves Moabite wives: the name of the one was Orpah and the name of the other was Ruth, and they lived there about ten years.
0:3.note: The Hebrew phrase "lifted up" echoes the same verb used for taking foreign wives in the Torah's warnings, quietly signaling a tension in this Moabite marriage story.

0:4.rendering: Then both Mahlon and Kilion also died, and Naomi was left without her two sons and without her husband.
0:4.note: The Hebrew repeats "also" and "without," hammering home the triple loss that strips Naomi of every male protector in a world where widows were vulnerable.

0:5.rendering: Then she arose with her daughters-in-law to return from the fields of Moab, for she had heard in the fields of Moab that the LORD had visited his people by giving them bread.
0:5.note: The Hebrew word for "visited" echoes God's decisive actions in Exodus, where he "visits" Israel to deliver them from famine and slavery.

0:6.rendering: So she set out from the place where she had been living, along with her two daughters-in-law, and they began the journey back to the land of Judah.
0:6.note: The Hebrew verb for "set out" echoes the same word used when Abraham left his homeland, hinting that this Moabite widow's return is also a pilgrimage of promise.

0:7.rendering: Then Naomi said to her two daughters-in-law, "Go, return each of you to your mother's house. May the Lord deal kindly with you as you have dealt with the dead and with me."
0:7.note: The Hebrew word for "deal kindly" is hesed, the same covenant loyalty that will echo through Ruth's pledge and ultimately point to the faithful redeemer in Bethlehem.

2:8.rendering: He said, "Who are you?" She answered, "I am Ruth, your servant. Spread the corner of your garment over your servant, for you are a family redeemer."
2:8.note: The phrase "spread the corner of your garment" literally echoes the Hebrew idiom for marriage, the same language God uses for covering Israel as his bride in Ezekiel 16:8.

2:9.rendering: He said, "May you be blessed by the Lord, my daughter. You have shown even more kindness now than before, since you did not go after young men, whether poor or rich."
2:9.note: The Hebrew word for kindness here is hesed, the same covenant loyalty Boaz praised in Ruth 2:12, now echoed back to her.

2:10.rendering: Now my daughter, do not be afraid. Everything you say I will do for you, for everyone in the gate of my people knows that you are a woman of noble character.
2:10.note: The word "noble character" echoes the exact phrase used of the ideal wife in Proverbs 31:10, quietly painting Ruth as the living embodiment of that wisdom poem.

2:11.rendering: And now, my daughter, do not fear. I will do for you everything that you ask, for all the gate of my people knows that you are a woman of noble character.
2:11.note: The phrase "woman of noble character" echoes the closing poem of Proverbs, where the same rare Hebrew word describes the ideal wife who fears the Lord.

2:12.rendering: Stay here tonight. Then in the morning, if he wants to redeem you, good. Let him redeem you. But if he is not willing to redeem you, then as surely as the Lord lives, I myself will redeem you. Lie down until morning.
2:12.note: The Hebrew word for "redeem" echoes the same term used for Boaz's earlier kindness, tying Ruth's story back to God's pattern of kinsman-redeemers.

2:13.rendering: So she lay at his feet until morning, but got up before anyone could recognize each other. Then he said, "Let it not be known that the woman came to the threshing floor."
2:13.note: The Hebrew literally says she arose "before a man could recognize his neighbor," a phrase that hints at the risky secrecy of this nighttime encounter on the threshing floor.

2:14.rendering: Then he said, "Hold out the cloak you are wearing and grasp it." She held it out, and he measured out six measures of barley and put it on her. Then he went into the city.
2:14.note: The six measures of barley echo the generous provision in Boaz's field earlier, hinting at the kinsman-redeemer's overflowing kindness.

2:15.rendering: When she came to her mother-in-law, Naomi asked, "Who are you, my daughter?" She told her all that the man had done for her.
2:15.note: Naomi's strange question "Who are you?" echoes her earlier cry to be called "Mara" instead of Naomi, hinting at the hope that Ruth's night encounter has already begun to restore her identity.

1:8.rendering: Keep your eyes on the field they are harvesting, and follow along after them. Indeed, I have ordered the young men not to touch you. And when you get thirsty, go to the jars and drink from what the young men have drawn.
1:8.note: The Hebrew verb for "ordered" carries the weight of a solemn charge, the same word used when God commands creation or a king issues a binding decree.

1:9.rendering: Then she fell on her face, bowed down to the ground, and said to him, "Why have I found favor in your eyes, that you should notice me, though I am a foreigner?"
1:9.note: Ruth's question echoes the Hebrew idiom for unexpected kindness, highlighting her vulnerable status as a Moabite outsider in a field full of Israelite workers.

1:10.rendering: Boaz answered her, "It has been fully reported to me all that you have done for your mother-in-law after the death of your husband, how you left your father and your mother and the land of your birth, and came to a people that you did not know before."
1:10.note: The Hebrew echoes Genesis 12:1, where God tells Abram to leave his father's house and country for an unknown land.

1:11.rendering: May the LORD reward your work, and may your wages be full from the LORD, the God of Israel, under whose wings you have come to take refuge.
1:11.note: The Hebrew word for "wings" (kanaph) echoes the protective cherubim wings over the ark in the tabernacle, portraying God as a fierce mother bird sheltering a foreign outsider.

1:12.rendering: Then she said, "May I continue to find favor in your eyes, my lord, for you have comforted me and spoken kindly to your servant, even though I am not one of your servant girls."
1:12.note: The Hebrew word for "favor" (hen) echoes back to the story's opening, where Ruth "found favor" in Naomi's eyes by choosing loyalty over security.

1:13.rendering: At mealtime Boaz said to her, "Come over here, eat some of the bread, and dip your piece in the vinegar." So she sat beside the reapers, and he passed her some roasted grain. She ate until she was satisfied, and had some left over.
1:13.note: The Hebrew word for "vinegar" here echoes the sour wine later offered to a suffering redeemer, quietly foreshadowing provision in the midst of hardship.

1:14.rendering: When Ruth got up to glean, Boaz commanded his young men: "Even if she gathers among the sheaves, do not humiliate her."
1:14.note: Boaz's command literally says "do not shame her," an ancient Near Eastern way of protecting a vulnerable foreign woman's dignity in the fields.

1:15.rendering: Even pull some stalks out of the bundles for her, and leave them for her to glean. Do not rebuke her.
1:15.note: The Hebrew verb for "pull out" is the same one used when Boaz "drew near" to Ruth, quietly hinting at his growing favor.

3:8.rendering: Then Boaz said to the elders and to all the people, "You are witnesses today that I have acquired from the hand of Naomi all that belonged to Elimelech, and all that belonged to Kilion and Mahlon."
3:8.note: Boaz's public declaration echoes the redemption laws of Leviticus 25, turning a family crisis into covenant community witness.

3:9.rendering: Moreover, I have acquired Ruth the Moabite, the wife of Mahlon, to be my wife, in order to raise up the name of the dead over his inheritance, so that the name of the dead will not be cut off from among his brothers and from the gate of his place. You are witnesses today.
3:9.note: This act echoes the levirate duty in Deuteronomy 25, yet Ruth the foreigner becomes the surprising vessel for preserving an Israelite name, weaving outsiders into Israel's family line.

3:10.rendering: Then all the people who were at the gate, along with the elders, said, "We are witnesses. May Yahweh make the woman who is coming into your house like Rachel and Leah, the two who built up the house of Israel. May you act worthily in Ephrathah and be renowned in Bethlehem."
3:10.note: The witnesses invoke Rachel and Leah, the mothers who "built" Israel's house, echoing the verb "banah" used for family expansion in Genesis.

3:11.rendering: May your house be like the house of Perez, whom Tamar bore to Judah, through the offspring that the Lord will give you by this young woman.
3:11.note: The blessing echoes the scandalous Perez birth in Genesis 38, where Tamar's bold act preserved Judah's line, hinting how Ruth's story weaves redemption from unlikely unions.

3:12.rendering: So Boaz took Ruth, and she became his wife. He went in to her, and the Lord enabled her to conceive, and she gave birth to a son.
3:12.note: The Hebrew verb "gave her conception" (natan herayon) echoes God's direct interventions in Genesis 29:31 and 30:22, quietly signaling that this Moabite outsider is now woven into Israel's covenant story.

3:13.rendering: Then the women said to Naomi, "Praise be to the LORD, who has not left you without a redeemer today. May his name be renowned in Israel!"
3:13.note: The women's blessing echoes the book's opening famine and emptiness, now reversed by a redeemer whose name will endure, a quiet setup for the Davidic line.

3:14.rendering: He will renew your life and sustain you in your old age. For your daughter-in-law, who loves you and is better to you than seven sons, has borne him.
3:14.note: The Hebrew word for "renew" echoes the name Obed, meaning "servant," hinting at how this child will serve Naomi's future in a story that quietly prepares the line for David.

3:15.rendering: Naomi took the child and laid him on her lap, and she became his nurse.
3:15.note: The Hebrew word for "nurse" (omenet) echoes the same root used for royal guardians and foster mothers in the ancient court, quietly signaling this child's place in the royal line.

0:8.rendering: May the LORD grant that you find rest, each of you in the house of her husband. Then she kissed them, and they lifted up their voices and wept.
0:8.note: The Hebrew word for "rest" here echoes the key theme of finding security and home, a longing that will drive the whole story toward unexpected provision.

0:9.rendering: They said to her, "We will go back with you to your people."
0:9.note: The daughters-in-law echo Ruth's later pledge of loyalty, but only Ruth will follow through to Bethlehem.

0:10.rendering: But Naomi said, "Turn back, my daughters. Why should you go with me? Do I still have sons in my womb that they might become your husbands?"
0:10.note: Naomi's question echoes the ancient custom of levirate marriage, where a brother-in-law raises up offspring for the deceased, yet here it twists with bitter irony since she has no more sons.

0:11.rendering: Turn back, my daughters. Go with me? I am too old to have a husband. Even if I said, 'I have hope,' even if I had a husband tonight and even bore sons,
0:11.note: The Hebrew word for "hope" here echoes the key term for Israel's future expectation, turning Naomi's despair into a quiet meditation on barren waiting.

0:12.rendering: Would you then wait until they were grown? Would you then shut yourselves off from having husbands? No, my daughters. It is far more bitter for me than for you, for the hand of the Lord has gone out against me.
0:12.note: The Hebrew word for "bitter" here echoes Naomi's later name change to Mara in 1:20, tying her personal grief to the Lord's hand against her.

0:13.rendering: Then they lifted up their voices and wept again. Orpah kissed her mother-in-law goodbye, but Ruth clung tightly to her.
0:13.note: The Hebrew word for "clung" is the same one used when a man leaves his parents and clings to his wife in Genesis 2:24, hinting that Ruth's fierce loyalty is forging a new kind of family bond.

0:14.rendering: Then Naomi said, "Look, your sister-in-law has gone back to her people and to her gods. Go back with her."
0:14.note: Naomi's words echo the ancient Near Eastern expectation that a widow would return to her father's household and its deities, setting up Ruth's radical counter-choice.

0:15.rendering: But Ruth said, "Do not press me to leave you, to turn back from following you. For where you go, I will go, and where you stay, I will stay. Your people shall be my people, and your God my God."
0:15.note: This pledge of loyalty echoes ancient covenant language, binding Ruth the Moabite to Naomi's people and God in a way that reshapes her identity forever.

2:16.rendering: She said, "He gave me these six measures of barley, for he told me, 'You must not return to your mother-in-law empty-handed.'"
2:16.note: The six measures of barley echo Boaz's earlier generosity to Ruth at the harvest, quietly signaling his commitment to redeem without a word spoken yet.

2:17.rendering: Then Naomi said, "Sit still, my daughter, until you know how the matter turns out. For the man will not rest unless he settles the matter today."
2:17.note: The Hebrew "menuchah" (rest) echoes the rest God gives his people, as Naomi urges Ruth to wait in quiet trust for Boaz to finish the redemption.

3:16.rendering: The neighboring women gave him a name, saying, "A son has been born to Naomi." They named him Obed. He is the father of Jesse, the father of David.
3:16.note: The name Obed means "servant," quietly echoing the loyal service of Ruth and Boaz that brings the Moabite outsider into Israel's royal line.

3:17.rendering: Now these are the generations of Perez: Perez fathered Hezron,
3:17.note: This genealogy quietly echoes the opening of Genesis, signaling how a Moabite widow's story has now been woven into Israel's royal family tree.

3:18.rendering: Hezron fathered Ram, and Ram fathered Amminadab.
3:18.note: This quiet link in the genealogy quietly bridges the exodus generation to the royal line, echoing how God weaves ordinary family trees into his larger story.

3:19.rendering: Nahshon fathered Salmon, Salmon fathered Boaz,
3:19.note: This link in the genealogy quietly ties the exodus generation (Nahshon was its prince) to the coming kingly line.

3:20.rendering: Salmon fathered Boaz, Boaz fathered Obed,
3:20.note: This quiet link in the genealogy echoes the name Boaz from the story's hero, tying Ruth's redemption to the line that will produce King David.

3:21.rendering: Obed fathered Jesse, and Jesse fathered David.
3:21.note: This final name quietly echoes the promise to Abraham, threading the Moabite outsider into the line that will birth Israel's greatest king.

1:16.rendering: So she gleaned in the field until evening. Then she beat out what she had gleaned, and it came to about an ephah of barley.
1:16.note: An ephah was roughly thirty pounds of grain, enough to feed a family for a week, showing Ruth's tireless work and the quiet abundance beginning to break into Naomi's emptiness.

1:17.rendering: She picked it up, went back into town, and her mother-in-law saw what she had gleaned. Ruth also took out what she had left over from her meal and gave it to her.
1:17.note: The Hebrew word for "left over" echoes the manna story in Exodus, where gathering extra food becomes an act of provision for others.

1:18.rendering: Her mother-in-law said to her, "Where have you gleaned today? And where have you worked? Blessed be the one who took notice of you." So she told her mother-in-law with whom she had worked, and said, "The name of the man with whom I worked today is Boaz."
1:18.note: The Hebrew word for "took notice" echoes the same root Naomi used earlier when she felt God had "noticed" her family's tragedy, now flipped into unexpected blessing.

1:19.rendering: Naomi said to her daughter-in-law, "May he be blessed by the Lord, whose kindness has not forsaken the living or the dead." And Naomi said to her, "The man is close to us, he is one of our redeemers."
1:19.note: The word "kindness" here is hesed, the same covenant loyalty that echoes God's promise to David centuries later.

1:20.rendering: Then Ruth the Moabite said, "He also told me, 'Stay close by my young men until they have finished all my harvest.'"
1:20.note: The Hebrew emphasizes "the Moabite" here, echoing the outsider status that makes Boaz's protective instruction all the more striking in this ancient harvest setting.

1:21.rendering: Naomi said to Ruth her daughter-in-law, "It is good, my daughter, that you go out with his young women, so that they do not meet you in any other field."
1:21.note: Naomi's advice echoes the protective language of ancient village life, where a foreign widow's safety hinged on staying within one clan's watchful gaze.

1:22.rendering: So she stayed close to the young women of Boaz, gleaning until the end of the barley harvest and the wheat harvest. Then she returned to her mother-in-law.
1:22.note: The word "stayed close" echoes the Hebrew for clinging that Ruth used of her loyalty to Naomi, now quietly fulfilled in the fields of her future redeemer.

0:16.rendering: Where you die, I will die, and there I will be buried. May the Lord do this to me and even more if even death separates me from you.
0:16.note: This vow echoes ancient Near Eastern treaty language, binding Ruth to Naomi with a solemn oath that death alone can break.

0:17.rendering: When Naomi saw that she was determined to go with her, she stopped trying to talk her out of it.
0:17.note: The Hebrew word for "determined" here is the same one used for God "setting his face" toward Jerusalem in Ezekiel  a stubborn resolve that echoes through the story.

0:18.rendering: So the two of them went on until they came to Bethlehem. And when they arrived in Bethlehem, the whole town was stirred because of them, and the women said, "Is this Naomi?"
0:18.note: The Hebrew word for "stirred" echoes the same verb used for the earthquake-like commotion in a city under threat, hinting that Naomi's return feels like a seismic disruption to the community.

0:19.rendering: She said to them, "Do not call me Naomi. Call me Mara, for the Almighty has dealt very bitterly with me."
0:19.note: The name change from Naomi (pleasant) to Mara (bitter) echoes the wilderness water crisis in Exodus 15:23, where bitter waters test God's people.

0:20.rendering: I left full, but YHWH has brought me back empty. Why call me Naomi, when YHWH has testified against me and Shaddai has brought calamity on me?
0:20.note: The wordplay on "Naomi" (pleasant) versus "Mara" (bitter) in the next verse echoes the name change of Abram to Abraham, showing how personal identity shifts under God's sovereign hand.

0:21.rendering: So Naomi returned, and Ruth the Moabite her daughter-in-law with her, who returned from the fields of Moab. They came to Bethlehem at the beginning of the barley harvest.
0:21.note: The barley harvest sets the stage for provision and new life, echoing how this Moabite outsider enters Israel's story at the exact moment of abundance.
